threats.title: LendingTerm._partialRepay minBorrow Check Uses Inconsistent Units After creditMultiplier Decrease
threats.hypothesis_description: In _partialRepay(), the remaining borrowAmount after partial repay is compared against `ProfitManager.minBorrow()`. However, borrowAmount is stored as the original CREDIT amount at borrow time, while minBorrow() returns `_minBorrow * 1e18 / creditMultiplier`. When creditMultiplier decreases (due to bad debt), minBorrow() increases, potentially exceeding the stored borrowAmount even though the loan's effective debt in peg terms is still well above the minimum. This blocks partial repayments entirely for affected loans.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unit mismatch between stored borrowAmount (original nominal CREDIT) and the dynamically-scaled minBorrow (current CREDIT) creates a scenario where valid partial repayments are blocked after creditMultiplier decreases.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(borrowAmount - issuanceDecrease > ProfitManager.minBorrow()) compares stored nominal amount against dynamically scaled minimum

preconditions: creditMultiplier has decreased from its value at loan origination
Loan's stored borrowAmount (original nominal) < current minBorrow() (scaled)
Borrower attempts partial repayment

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: borrowAmount is stored nominal CREDIT from borrow time. minBorrow() = _minBorrow * 1e18 / creditMultiplier grows as creditMultiplier shrinks. A loan with borrowAmount=150e18 opened at multiplier=1e18 cannot partial repay if multiplier drops to 0.5e18 (minBorrow becomes 200e18 > 150e18).
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No scaling of borrowAmount to current creditMultiplier for comparison

analysis_questions: With _minBorrow=100e18, at what creditMultiplier does partial repay become impossible for loans of various sizes?
Are borrowers forced to fully repay or face liquidation?
Is the strict > (instead of >=) an additional issue?
validation_requirements: Calculate threshold creditMultiplier for various loan sizes
Verify partial repay reverts when borrowAmount < minBorrow() after multiplier decrease
Test with concrete values showing the DoS

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: System experiences bad debt, creditMultiplier drops from 1e18 to 0.5e18. All existing loans with borrowAmount below 200e18 (original nominal) can no longer do partial repayments. These borrowers must either fully repay or face liquidation when partial repay delays pass.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Borrowers forced into full repayment or liquidation unnecessarily
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All borrowers with smaller loans after creditMultiplier decrease

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for partial repay after creditMultiplier decrease

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: N/A - triggered by normal protocol operations
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable when partial repay transactions start reverting

threats.title: ERC20Gauges._decrementWeightUntilFree Infinite Loop When User Has Zero-Weight Gauge
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC20Gauges._decrementWeightUntilFree(), the loop counter `i` is only incremented inside the `if (userGaugeWeight != 0)` block. If a user has a gauge with 0 weight in their gauge set (added via incrementGauge(gauge, 0)), and the loop reaches that gauge before freeing enough weight, it will loop infinitely on the zero-weight entry, causing an out-of-gas revert. This blocks all token transfers and burns for the affected user.
threats.investigation_rationale: The incrementGauge function does not validate weight > 0, allowing a gauge to be added to the user's set with 0 weight. The greedy decrement algorithm assumes all gauges in the set have positive weight.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { ++i; } only inside if (userGaugeWeight != 0) block; zero-weight gauge causes infinite iteration

preconditions: User calls incrementGauge(gauge, 0), adding gauge to their set with 0 weight
User has other gauge allocations that consume their balance
User attempts to transfer or burn tokens requiring weight freeing

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Loop counter increment inside conditional block
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The for loop at line 484 only increments i (line 498) when userGaugeWeight != 0. A gauge with 0 weight causes the same index to be checked repeatedly, creating an infinite loop.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No weight > 0 check in incrementGauge; no unconditional i++ in loop

analysis_questions: Can an attacker cause this on the SurplusGuildMinter if mintRatio is set to 0?
Is incrementGauge(gauge, 0) actually reachable given GuildToken override checks?
Can the user recover by manually decrementing the zero-weight gauge?
validation_requirements: Test incrementGauge(gauge, 0) succeeds and adds to gauge set
Test subsequent transfer/burn reverts with out of gas
Verify if decrementGauge(gauge, 0) can remove the problematic entry

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User calls GuildToken.incrementGauge(gauge, 0). The gauge is added to their _userGauges set with 0 weight. Later, when the user tries to transfer tokens that require weight freeing, _decrementWeightUntilFree enters an infinite loop on the zero-weight gauge, consuming all gas.
prerequisites: User (or protocol acting on user's behalf) calls incrementGauge with weight=0

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: User's GUILD tokens permanently locked - cannot transfer or burn
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Self-inflicted for direct users; could affect SurplusGuildMinter if mintRatio=0
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: User may recover by calling decrementGauge(gauge, 0) to remove the entry

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for incrementGauge with zero weight followed by transfer

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Loop will consume all gas in block
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: User transactions revert with out of gas

threats.title: ProfitManager.notifyPnL Reverts on Large Loss Due to Underflow in creditMultiplier Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: In ProfitManager.notifyPnL(), during loss handling, after depleting the surplus buffer, the remaining loss is used to update creditMultiplier: `newCreditMultiplier = (creditMultiplier * (creditTotalSupply - loss)) / creditTotalSupply`. If the remaining loss exceeds creditTotalSupply, the subtraction underflows in Solidity 0.8.13 (checked arithmetic), causing a revert. This means the loss can never be processed, blocking the auction settlement and locking collateral permanently.
threats.investigation_rationale: When creditMultiplier drops from prior losses, the principal calculation `borrowAmount * borrowCreditMultiplier / creditMultiplier` amplifies the loss. If this amplified principal exceeds creditTotalSupply after surplus buffer depletion, the system enters an irrecoverable state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 newCreditMultiplier = (creditMultiplier * (creditTotalSupply - loss)) / creditTotalSupply; underflows when loss > creditTotalSupply

preconditions: creditMultiplier has decreased significantly from prior losses
A loan opened at a higher creditMultiplier defaults
The amplified principal (borrowAmount * oldMultiplier / currentMultiplier) exceeds creditTotalSupply after buffer depletion

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked subtraction potential underflow in Solidity 0.8.13
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: (creditTotalSupply - loss) can underflow when loss is amplified by creditMultiplier ratio
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No clamping of loss to creditTotalSupply; no floor on creditMultiplier

analysis_questions: What creditMultiplier ratio causes the amplified principal to exceed totalSupply?
Can this state be reached through cascading liquidations?
What happens to the auction and collateral when notifyPnL reverts?
validation_requirements: Calculate threshold creditMultiplier for various loan sizes
Trace the full auction -> onBid -> notifyPnL path to confirm revert blocks settlement
Verify collateral is permanently locked in LendingTerm

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Multiple losses reduce creditMultiplier to 0.01e18. A loan opened at creditMultiplier=1e18 with borrowAmount=X defaults. Principal = X * 100. If X*100 > creditTotalSupply - surplusBuffer, notifyPnL reverts. The auction cannot settle via bid(), forgive() also fails. The loan and its collateral are permanently stuck.
prerequisites: Large loan default relative to current total supply

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: System enters irrecoverable state; affected loan collateral permanently locked; credit system potentially bricked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol participants if system enters death spiral
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Only through governor emergencyAction to manually patch state

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for cascading losses with creditMultiplier approaching extreme values

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Requires significant prior bad debt to reduce creditMultiplier

threats.title: ProfitManager Guild Profit Permanently Stuck When Gauge Weight Is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: In ProfitManager.notifyPnL(), when profit is distributed, amountForGuild is calculated and subtracted from the total to compute amountForCredit. However, if the gauge's weight is 0 at the time of profit notification, the gaugeProfitIndex is not updated, and the CREDIT tokens allocated to GUILD stakers remain stuck in ProfitManager with no claimant. This can occur when a term is offboarded (gauge deprecated, weight removed from totals) but auctions from that term still settle with profit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code path for guild profit distribution silently skips index update when gauge weight is 0, but the CREDIT tokens have already been deducted from the creditSplit. These tokens become permanently unrecoverable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_gaugeWeight != 0) { update profit index } -- amountForGuild CREDIT stays in ProfitManager when weight is 0

preconditions: A gauge has weight 0 when profit is notified (e.g., term offboarded)
guildSplit in profitSharingConfig is > 0
An auction settles with profit for a deprecated term

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Conditional profit index update with unconditional profit split deduction
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: amountForGuild is computed and subtracted from total regardless of gauge weight. When weight is 0, index not updated, tokens stuck.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No redirection of guild split to surplus buffer or credit holders when weight is 0

analysis_questions: How much CREDIT can accumulate in ProfitManager from this path?
Can governance recover these funds via emergencyAction?
Is there a timing window during offboarding where this consistently occurs?
validation_requirements: Verify amountForGuild is subtracted from profit even when gaugeWeight is 0
Trace CREDIT balance of ProfitManager after profit notification with 0-weight gauge
Confirm no claim mechanism exists for these orphaned funds

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A term is offboarded and its gauge weight drops to 0. Ongoing auctions for that term settle with profit. The guild share of profit is allocated but not distributed to any profit index. CREDIT tokens accumulate in ProfitManager without any claim path.
prerequisites: Auction settles with profit after offboarding

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: CREDIT tokens permanently locked in ProfitManager proportional to guildSplit * total profits from 0-weight terms
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol treasury; indirectly all CREDIT holders who lose their share
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governor could recover via emergencyAction on ProfitManager

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for profit notification on deprecated gauge

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Occurs naturally during offboarding + auction settlement
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing ProfitManager CREDIT balance to surplusBuffer + pending rewards

threats.title: ERC20RebaseDistributor updateTotalRebasingShares Can Overflow uint224 Trapping Last Rebasers
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC20RebaseDistributor.updateTotalRebasingShares(), when shares decrease significantly (most rebasers exit), the adjusted target share price `targetNewSharePrice = currentRebasingSharePrice + (delta * START_REBASING_SHARE_PRICE) / percentChange` can overflow uint224 if percentChange is very small. This happens because dividing by a tiny percentChange amplifies the delta. SafeCastLib.safeCastTo224 would revert, blocking the remaining rebaser from exiting rebase.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula adjusts the remaining interpolation delta inversely to the share change. When shares decrease dramatically (e.g., from 1000 to 1), the multiplier 1000x is applied to the remaining delta, potentially exceeding uint224 max.

proof_of_signal.pattern: targetNewSharePrice = currentPrice + (delta * START_REBASING_SHARE_PRICE) / percentChange; percentChange can be very small, amplifying result beyond uint224

preconditions: Active interpolation with delta between current and target share price > 0
Most rebasers exit, leaving very few shares (percentChange becomes very small)
The amplified targetNewSharePrice exceeds uint224 max (~2.7e67)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Inverse proportional scaling without overflow guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: percentChange = (sharesAfter * 1e30) / sharesBefore. If sharesAfter << sharesBefore, percentChange is tiny. targetNewSharePrice = currentPrice + (delta * 1e30) / percentChange can exceed 2^224.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No capping of targetNewSharePrice before safeCastTo224

analysis_questions: What is the minimum share ratio that causes overflow?
Can this occur organically or only through manipulation?
Are the remaining trapped rebasers' funds permanently locked?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact threshold: delta and share ratio that causes uint224 overflow
Verify _exitRebase path goes through updateTotalRebasingShares with negative sharesDelta
Check if waiting for interpolation to complete (delta = 0) allows safe exit

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Large distribution occurs, creating significant delta in share price interpolation. Most rebasers exit (reducing shares by 99%). The remaining delta is amplified 100x, potentially exceeding uint224. The last few rebasers cannot exit because updateTotalRebasingShares reverts on safeCastTo224.
prerequisites: Active share price interpolation with non-zero delta

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Remaining rebasers' CREDIT tokens trapped in rebasing state
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May recover after interpolation completes (delta goes to 0) or via forceExitRebase

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for share price adjustment with dramatic share decrease during active interpolation

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Requires controlling most of rebasing supply
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Must exit during active interpolation period

threats.title: PSM decimalCorrection may underflow / revert for peg tokens with >18 decimals, blocking PSM deployment or bricking mint/redeem paths
threats.hypothesis_description: SimplePSM computes `decimalCorrection = 10 ** (18 - decimals)` using `ERC20(_pegToken).decimals()`. If the configured peg token reports decimals > 18 (or a malicious token returns an unexpected value), the subtraction underflows and deployment reverts, or the PSM becomes unusable. This is a concrete code defect: no bounds check on decimals.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if intended peg tokens are 6/18 decimals, a misconfigured deployment (or token upgrade changing decimals semantics) can permanently prevent minting/redemption, breaking the peg mechanism and impacting `ProfitManager.totalBorrowedCredit()` assumptions.
deployment_assumptions: The deployed pegToken has decimals() > 18 OR can change behavior post-deploy (upgradeable token) to report >18
Protocol relies on SimplePSM mint/redeem for peg maintenance
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script wiring/validation is provided for SimplePSM pegToken decimals bounds; SimplePSM constructor trusts ERC20(_pegToken).decimals() without range checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: decimalCorrection = 10 ** (18 - decimals); subtraction underflows if decimals>18

preconditions: SimplePSM is deployed/configured with a pegToken whose decimals() returns > 18 (or changes to >18)
No external wrapper enforces pegToken decimals <= 18

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unchecked assumption about ERC20.decimals() range leading to underflow in exponent base
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: PSM deployment or operation can revert entirely for misconfigured/malicious peg token

analysis_questions: Is pegToken guaranteed by governance process to have decimals <= 18 on all target chains?
Is pegToken upgradeable (can decimals() behavior change)?
Is there any deployment-time validation elsewhere for pegToken decimals bounds?
validation_requirements: Unit test deploying SimplePSM with decimals 19/24 to confirm revert
Review governance/onboarding procedures for pegToken selection/verification

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker (or misconfiguration) causes deployment/configuration of a PSM with a token reporting >18 decimals; PSM cannot be deployed or becomes non-functional, breaking mint/redeem utility and potentially destabilizing the CREDIT peg.
prerequisites: Governance/config error OR pegToken is upgradeable/malicious

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Peg mechanism unavailable; could materially impact protocol stability and user redemptions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All CREDIT holders relying on PSM liquidity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires redeploy/migrate PSM or change peg token

threats.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of tests ensuring decimals <= 18 or safe scaling behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Check deployed pegToken decimals and upgradeability

threats.title: Loan ID generation uses timestamp-only entropy causing same-second self-DoS and potential cross-user collision scenarios under account abstraction batching
threats.hypothesis_description: LendingTerm._borrow derives `loanId = keccak256(abi.encode(borrower, address(this), block.timestamp))` and permanently prevents re-use because `borrowTime` stays non-zero after close. This makes it impossible for the same borrower to open two loans in the same second on the same term (even if the first is repaid later), and can cause user-facing DoS for smart wallets/batch executors that submit multiple borrows within the same timestamp.
threats.investigation_rationale: On L2s with centralized sequencers and potentially coarse timestamp behavior, same-second batching is common. Practical attackers can grief themselves/others by inducing revert patterns in batched flows (e.g., a contract that tries to open multiple positions atomically). While not a fund loss, it can break integrator strategies and produce stuck workflows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: loanId = keccak256(abi.encode(borrower,address(this),block.timestamp)); require(loans[loanId].borrowTime == 0)

preconditions: Borrower (EOA or smart wallet) attempts multiple borrows within same block.timestamp on same term
No alternative nonce/salt included in loanId

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: identifier derived solely from timestamp, with non-resetting existence marker
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: prevents same-second multiple loans; potential integration DoS
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no user nonce, sequential counter, or monotonic loan index

analysis_questions: Is one-loan-per-second an intended constraint or an accidental limitation?
Do any protocol components (routers, batchers) attempt multiple borrows per second for same borrower?
validation_requirements: Test two borrows in same second from same borrower; confirm second reverts
Assess L2 timestamp granularity and likelihood of same-second batching

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A smart wallet executes a batch intended to open multiple loans on the same term within one transaction/second; the second borrow reverts due to loanId collision, causing the entire batch to fail and potentially blocking dependent actions.
prerequisites: Use of batching/account abstraction or automated strategies

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: strategy failure, missed opportunities; no direct theft
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Workaround by spacing transactions; code change required for full fix

threats.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of tests for multiple borrows per second behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor reverts with 'loan exists' on borrow

threats.title: SurplusGuildMinter.updateMintRatio is permissionless and can be griefed to trigger large rate-limited mints/burns against the system's mint buffers
threats.hypothesis_description: SurplusGuildMinter.updateMintRatio(user, term) is external and permissionless; it can mint/burn potentially large amounts of GUILD (via RateLimitedMinter and GuildToken gauge voting) to align a user's position with the current mintRatio. An attacker can repeatedly call updateMintRatio for many users/terms to consume rate-limit buffer (mint path) or trigger large burns/replenishments, impacting other legitimate protocol operations that depend on the same rate limited minter buffer.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if not directly profitable, this is a practical on-chain grief vector: spending gas to force protocol-wide scarcity of rate-limited mint capacity can delay onboarding, staking, or other flows that require rate-limited minting, depending on how rlgm is shared.
deployment_assumptions: The RateLimitedMinter `rlgm` used by SurplusGuildMinter is shared with other critical operations (or has tight buffer settings)
There exist many users with stakes such that mintRatio changes create large deltas
threats.config_evidence: No deployment wiring for rlgm isolation is provided; SurplusGuildMinter stores immutable rlgm and calls RateLimitedMinter(rlgm).mint/replenishBuffer in updateMintRatio().

proof_of_signal.pattern: external permissionless updateMintRatio() triggers RateLimitedMinter.mint/replenishBuffer and gauge weight changes for arbitrary user

preconditions: At least one user has an active stake for `term` (userStake.stakeTime != 0)
mintRatio has changed such that guildAfter != guildBefore for many users
RateLimitedMinter buffer is finite and can be exhausted

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless state-changing function that can force expensive mint/burn side effects for arbitrary users
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: rate-limited buffer griefing; forced heavy operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no access control; no per-user opt-in; no rate-limit at SGM layer; no cap on per-call delta

analysis_questions: Is rlgm dedicated exclusively to SGM or shared with other protocol mint flows?
What are typical bufferCap/rateLimitPerSecond for rlgm, and could they be exhausted by third parties calling updateMintRatio?
Can an attacker select users/terms to maximize mint delta per call?

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: after mintRatio changes or whenever stakes are large
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor unusual updateMintRatio call volume and rate limiter buffer drops

threats.title: ProfitManager.initializeReferences is a one-time assert-based initializer; mis-ordering or partial setup can permanently brick core accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: ProfitManager.initializeReferences uses `assert(credit==0 && guild==0 && psm==0)` (not `require`) and sets critical addresses. If called with wrong addresses, or if one of those references is accidentally set in a prior transaction (even to a non-zero incorrect value), the contract becomes permanently misconfigured without an in-code recovery path. Because it uses `assert`, failures consume all gas and indicate an invariant violation, complicating ops and monitoring.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is configuration-dependent but high impact: ProfitManager drives creditMultiplier, totalBorrowedCredit, profit distribution, and loss socialization. A bricked/miswired ProfitManager breaks most protocol invariants and can lock core functionality (borrows, PSM calculations, debt ceilings).
deployment_assumptions: ProfitManager.initializeReferences is called post-deploy (as intended) and could be called with incorrect parameters or in the wrong sequence
No immutable wiring exists for these references
threats.config_evidence: Provided deployment script (DeployProposal.s.sol) does not show protocol wiring steps; no evidence initializeReferences is called correctly/atomically with verified addresses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: assert(credit==0 && guild==0 && psm==0); sets core references with no later setter

preconditions: initializeReferences has not been called yet OR is called incorrectly
Governance/operator error or compromised privileged role (GOVERNOR) triggers misconfiguration

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: critical one-time initialization with assert and no recovery setter
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no ability to update references; no require() with descriptive error; no two-step verification

analysis_questions: Is initializeReferences executed via timelock with verification checks in proposal scripts?
Are credit/guild/psm addresses immutable in practice (single deployment) or expected to migrate?
Is there an emergency migration path (e.g., setCore/emergencyAction) intended to replace ProfitManager usage?

Simulate misconfiguration and assess downstream breakages

threats.title: GuildVetoGovernor.executeVeto may allow executing cancel without quorum if OZ Governor state transitions mark proposal as executable before custom state() indicates Succeeded
threats.hypothesis_description: GuildVetoGovernor customizes vote counting such that only 'Against' votes exist and quorum is based on againstVotes, but it calls `super.execute(...)` in executeVeto without an explicit local check that `_quorumReached(proposalId)` is true at execution time. threats.investigation_rationale: This is a subtle integration risk between OZ Governor core and a heavily customized state machine. If exploitable, it allows unprivileged cancellation of timelock operations (governance grief / censorship). Must be validated by tracing OZ Governor's execute() path and whether it relies on `_quorumReached` and `_voteSucceeded` in the same way as state().
deployment_assumptions: GuildVetoGovernor is used in production and has permission to cancel operations in the linked timelock
OZ Governor version semantics match the expected invariants for execute()
threats.config_evidence: No explicit additional guard in executeVeto; relies on OZ Governor internals and custom overrides.

proof_of_signal.pattern: executeVeto calls super.execute(...) with no explicit quorum/state check in this wrapper

preconditions: A timelock operation exists and is pending
A veto proposal corresponding to that timelockId exists or can be created
There is a mismatch between OZ execute gating and the intended custom quorum-based success condition

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: delegating security condition to inherited framework without explicit local enforcement
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: potential cancel execution without reaching veto quorum

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Potentially re-queue actions; depends on timelock tooling

threats.title: SurplusGuildMinter getRewards() Checks Slashed Flag Against Uninitialized Memory, Enabling Permissionless Theft of All Staked CREDIT
threats.hypothesis_description: In SurplusGuildMinter.getRewards(), the `slashed` condition is evaluated against the default-initialized (zero) `userStake` return variable BEFORE the actual user stake is loaded from storage. Since `userStake.lastGaugeLoss` is 0 in the uninitialized struct, any term where `GuildToken.lastGaugeLoss(term) > 0` will cause `slashed = true` for ALL stakers, regardless of when they staked. This zeroes the user's stake record, permanently locking their CREDIT in the term surplus buffer and orphaning their GUILD gauge weight.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect where reading a return variable before it's populated from storage causes incorrect slash processing. The function is public and callable by anyone for any user, making it a permissionless griefing/theft vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: slashed flag evaluated against default-zero userStake.lastGaugeLoss before storage load at line 228: `userStake = _stakes[user][term]`

preconditions: A lending term has experienced at least one gauge loss (lastGaugeLoss > 0)
At least one user has an active stake in SurplusGuildMinter for that term
No special roles required - function is public

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Line 220: `if (lastGaugeLoss > uint256(userStake.lastGaugeLoss))` where userStake is still default-zero memory
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call getRewards(victim, term) for any victim on any term with historical loss
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker does not profit directly but permanently destroys victim CREDIT stakes and orphans GUILD weight; griefing attack with 100% victim loss

economic_questions: What is the total value at risk across all SurplusGuildMinter stakes on terms with historical losses?
Can the orphaned GUILD weight cause secondary effects on debt ceilings?
Is there any recovery mechanism for the locked termSurplusBuffer CREDIT?

threats.title: Stale canOffboard Flag Allows Immediate Re-Offboarding After Re-Onboarding, Permanently Pausing PSM Redemptions
threats.hypothesis_description: When a lending term is offboarded, `canOffboard[term]` is set to true and `nOffboardingsInProgress` is incremented. If the term is re-onboarded by governance (via addGauge) before cleanup() is called, `canOffboard[term]` persists as true. An attacker can immediately call `offboard(term)` again without any new poll or quorum, bypassing the democratic offboarding process. This double-increments `nOffboardingsInProgress` but cleanup() can only decrement it once (setting `canOffboard[term] = false`), leaving a permanent residual that keeps PSM redemptions paused.
threats.investigation_rationale: The canOffboard mapping is never reset when a term is re-onboarded, creating a state inconsistency. Combined with the nOffboardingsInProgress counter tracking PSM pause state, this creates a permanent DoS on PSM redemptions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: offboard() only checks canOffboard[term] which persists from previous offboarding cycle; no check that the term is currently in an active offboarding round

preconditions: A term was previously offboarded (canOffboard = true)
Governance re-onboarded the term without cleanup being called first
Attacker can call offboard() - no special roles needed

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: nOffboardingsInProgress incremented in offboard() but only decremented in cleanup() which sets canOffboard=false, preventing further decrements
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: offboard(term) called with stale canOffboard[term]=true after re-onboarding
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Permanent PSM redemption pause locks all CREDIT holders from redeeming for peg tokens; attacker profits by shorting CREDIT or holding collateral positions

Call cleanup -> verify nOffboardingsInProgress is 1 and canOffboard is false
Verify no path to decrement nOffboardingsInProgress further

threats.title: GuildVetoGovernor Deterministic ProposalId Prevents Repeated Veto Attempts on Same Timelock Action
threats.hypothesis_description: GuildVetoGovernor.createVeto() generates a deterministic proposalId from the timelockId (via the OZ Governor's propose mechanism hashing targets/values/calldatas/description). Since all these parameters are deterministic for a given timelockId, the same proposalId is generated each time. If a veto proposal fails to reach quorum (state = Defeated), OZ Governor prevents creating a new proposal with the same proposalId. An attacker can create a veto proposal early when few votes are available, ensuring it fails, permanently preventing legitimate veto attempts on that timelock action.
threats.investigation_rationale: The veto mechanism is critical for the honest minority security assumption. If vetos can be burned by early creation, the entire governance security model is undermined. Any malicious timelock action could be protected from veto.

proof_of_signal.pattern: createVeto builds deterministic proposal parameters from timelockId; OZ Governor prevents duplicate proposalIds; once Defeated, no retry possible

preconditions: A timelock action is pending (timelockExecutionTime > 1)

threats.title: ProfitSharingConfig Precision Loss from uint32 Truncation Causes Systematic Profit Misdistribution
threats.hypothesis_description: ProfitManager.setProfitSharingConfig() validates that splits sum to 1e18 at 18-decimal precision, then stores them as uint32 values after dividing by 1e9. This truncation loses up to (1e9 - 1) per split. When splits are read back via getProfitSharingConfig(), they're multiplied by 1e9, and creditSplit is computed as the residual (1e18 - surplusBufferSplit - guildSplit - otherSplit). The truncation means the actual distribution differs from configured intent, with the residual creditSplit absorbing the error. Over many distributions, this systematic error compounds.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable precision error in the storage packing optimization. While individual impact per distribution is small, it represents incorrect code that accumulates over the protocol's lifetime.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint32(surplusBufferSplit / 1e9) truncates; configured splits summing to 1e18 may not reconstruct accurately after storage round-trip

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: creditSplit absorbs truncation error; up to ~3e9 total rounding error across 3 stored splits per distribution

Over protocol lifetime, what is the cumulative impact?

threats.title: creditMultiplier Approaching Zero Bricks Multiple System Functions via Division by Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: creditMultiplier has no floor protection and can reach extremely low values or theoretically 0 through cascading losses. If creditMultiplier approaches 0: (1) getLoanDebt divides by creditMultiplier, amplifying all debts toward infinity; (2) SimplePSM.getMintAmountOut divides by creditMultiplier, allowing infinite CREDIT minting for minimal peg tokens; (3) minBorrow() divides by creditMultiplier, potentially overflowing; (4) the multiplier update formula itself can reach 0 if loss exactly equals totalSupply. At or near 0, the entire system becomes non-functional.
threats.investigation_rationale: The creditMultiplier is a critical system parameter used as a divisor in multiple calculations. The absence of a minimum floor creates a systemic risk from cascading bad debt events.

proof_of_signal.pattern: newCreditMultiplier has no floor; if it reaches 0, division by creditMultiplier in getLoanDebt (LendingTerm.sol:243) and getMintAmountOut (SimplePSM.sol:92) causes revert

threats.title: Perpetual Yield Delay via Micro-Distributions in Rebase Distributor
threats.hypothesis_description: The `ERC20RebaseDistributor.distribute()` function resets the 30-day interpolation period (`DISTRIBUTION_PERIOD`) for ALL pending unminted rewards every time it is called. An attacker can repeatedly call `distribute(1 wei)` every few hours. Each call takes the current unminted rewards and stretches them over a new 30-day period, perpetually flattening the distribution curve and preventing rebasing CREDIT holders from ever receiving their full accumulated yield.
threats.investigation_rationale: This mechanism allows a low-capital attacker to indefinitely lock the yield of all lenders, breaking the core economic incentive of the protocol. Validation requires modeling the interpolation curve under frequent micro-distributions.

threats.title: PSM Peg Denial of Service via Strategic Term Offboarding
threats.hypothesis_description: `LendingTermOffboarding.offboard()` pauses `SimplePSM` redemptions. Redemptions are only unpaused in `cleanup()`, which requires `LendingTerm.issuance() == 0`. An attacker with enough GUILD to reach the offboarding quorum can pass an offboard poll for any active term, immediately pausing the PSM. Because `cleanup()` requires all loans to be fully called and auctioned (which takes time and depends on market liquidity), the PSM peg mechanism is completely disabled for an extended period, breaking the stablecoin's peg.
threats.investigation_rationale: The PSM is critical for maintaining the CREDIT token peg. Tying its availability to the potentially lengthy loan liquidation process creates a massive systemic vulnerability. Validation requires testing the time delay between offboard and cleanup.

threats.title: Profit Index Dust Accumulation Permanently Leaks CREDIT
threats.hypothesis_description: In `ProfitManager.notifyPnL()`, the `gaugeProfitIndex` is incremented by `(amountForGuild * 1e18) / _gaugeWeight`. Because this division truncates the remainder, a small amount of CREDIT is left unaccounted for in the index. Over thousands of loan repayments across many terms, this truncated 'dust' accumulates in the `ProfitManager` contract. Since there is no sweep or recovery function for this dust, the CREDIT tokens are permanently leaked from the circulating supply.

threats.title: Whitelisted Contract Gas Limit DoS via Unlimited Delegates
threats.hypothesis_description: `ERC20MultiVotes` allows governance to whitelist contracts to bypass `maxDelegates` via `setContractExceedMaxDelegates`. If a whitelisted contract accumulates a massive number of delegates, any attempt to transfer or burn its tokens will trigger `_decrementVotesUntilFree()`, which loops over all delegates. threats.title: Debt Ceiling Check in _decrementGaugeWeight Prevents Slashing of GUILD Voters on Fully-Utilized Terms
threats.hypothesis_description: GuildToken._decrementGaugeWeight() requires that after decrementing weight, the term's issuance does not exceed the new debt ceiling: `require(issuance <= debtCeilingAfterDecrement)`. This check also applies during applyGaugeLoss(), which calls _decrementGaugeWeight to remove the voter's weight. If a term's issuance is at or near its debt ceiling, reducing gauge weight would drop the ceiling below issuance, causing the require to revert. This prevents slashing of GUILD voters who should be penalized for a loss-generating term, defeating the core slashing mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The slashing mechanism's correctness depends on applyGaugeLoss being callable. The debt ceiling check creates a circular dependency: voters can't be slashed because their weight maintains the debt ceiling that prevents slashing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _decrementGaugeWeight checks LendingTerm(gauge).debtCeiling(-int256(weight)) against issuance; applyGaugeLoss calls _decrementGaugeWeight; if issuance >= debtCeilingAfterDecrement, revert blocks slashing

preconditions: A term has generated bad debt (loss notified via notifyPnL)
The term's issuance is at or near its current debt ceiling
Loans on the term haven't been fully repaid/called yet

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: applyGaugeLoss -> _decrementGaugeWeight -> debtCeiling check creates circular dependency where slashing requires reducing weight but reducing weight requires issuance below ceiling

threats.title: Permissionless re-initialization of LendingTerm clones allows attacker to rewrite core/refs/params and take over collateral + mint paths
threats.hypothesis_description: LendingTerm.initialize() is externally callable with only an assert-based one-time guard that checks `address(core()) == address(0)`. For EIP-1167 clones, storage is zeroed at deployment, so any uninitialized clone can be initialized by an attacker with attacker-controlled `_core`, `refs` and `params`. If such a clone is later (mistakenly) onboarded, or users interact with it before official initialization, the attacker can redirect minting/creditToken/profitManager/auctionHouse addresses and collateral token, enabling theft of collateral and/or arbitrary CREDIT mint flows depending on chosen references.
threats.investigation_rationale: Initialization of clones is a common operational footgun. Even if the factory initializes atomically, any other pathway that creates a clone (or any deployment failure leaving an uninitialized term address known) becomes an immediate takeover vector. This is a code-level risk because initialize has no access control.
deployment_assumptions: At least one LendingTerm clone address exists on-chain in an uninitialized state (core==0) and is discoverable/usable
Users or governance might interact with that clone (send collateral, onboard it, or grant it roles) after attacker initialization
threats.deployment_evidence: Factory initializes clones in createTerm(), but no deployment evidence is provided that ONLY the factory can create clones or that off-chain processes never publish uninitialized clone addresses.

Attacker can call initialize() before legitimate initializer
Downstream systems accept this term address (e.g., users deposit collateral, or governance grants roles/onboards term)

threats.title: Auction callback trust + lack of reentrancy guards may allow malicious collateral tokens to reenter and corrupt loan/issuance via external calls during onBid/repay paths
threats.hypothesis_description: LendingTerm interacts with arbitrary collateral tokens via SafeERC20.safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom in _borrow/_addCollateral/_repay/onBid. There are no reentrancy guards. While the protocol documentation says rebasing/fee-on-transfer collateral is excluded, a malicious ERC20 collateral (or an upgradeable collateral token later upgraded) could execute callbacks (e.g., ERC777-style hooks or crafted ERC20 with external calls) during transfer to reenter into LendingTerm methods (borrow/addCollateral/repay/call) and attempt state corruption or denial of service. Even if core state updates occur before external calls in some paths, multiple cross-calls (ProfitManager.notifyPnL, RateLimitedMinter.replenishBuffer, CreditToken.burn) make the overall flow complex and may expose unexpected reentrancy surfaces.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical integration risk because collateralToken is configurable per term and validated only by a `totalSupply()` static call at term creation. If a malicious/upgradeable token is ever whitelisted as collateral, it can become an on-chain exploit vector without requiring mempool MEV.
deployment_assumptions: A LendingTerm is configured with a collateral token that can execute reentrancy (malicious ERC20 or ERC777-like behavior), or an upgradeable collateral token is later upgraded to add reentrancy
The reentrant call can reach a sensitive function and bypass expected sequencing
threats.config_evidence: LendingTermOnboarding.createTerm only checks collateralToken.totalSupply() call success; does not enforce non-upgradeable/non-callback token semantics.

threats.title: Strict minBorrow Check Prevents Exact Partial Repayments
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_partialRepay` function uses a strict greater-than (`>`) check against `minBorrow`, preventing borrowers from reducing their loan balance to exactly the minimum allowed amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `LendingTerm._partialRepay`, the check `require(borrowAmount - issuanceDecrease > ProfitManager(refs.profitManager).minBorrow(), "LendingTerm: below min borrow");` ensures the remaining loan size is valid. However, because it uses `>`, a borrower cannot leave exactly `minBorrow` debt. They must leave at least `minBorrow + 1`. This is a logic error that creates a small unusable range of debt.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Strict inequality used where inclusive inequality is expected

preconditions: A borrower attempts to partially repay their loan down to the minimum allowed size

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A borrower uses an automated strategy to maintain their loan size at the minimum allowed to maximize capital efficiency elsewhere. The strategy calculates the exact repayment needed to reach `minBorrow`. The transaction reverts, forcing the strategy to either fail or over-repay (fully closing the loan).
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary value tests for partial repayments

threats.title: Governor forgive() Breaks Active Auctions
threats.hypothesis_description: If a governor calls `forgive()` on a loan that is currently in an active auction, the loan is closed but the auction remains open permanently, bricking the AuctionHouse migration process.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `LendingTerm`, `forgive()` sets `closeTime = block.timestamp` but does not check if `callTime != 0`. If the loan is in auction, `AuctionHouse` expects to call `onBid` to settle it. However, `onBid` requires `closeTime == 0`. The auction can never be settled, meaning `nAuctionsInProgress` in `AuctionHouse` will never decrement. This permanently blocks `setAuctionHouse`, which requires `nAuctionsInProgress == 0`.

preconditions: A loan is called and enters an active auction
Governor calls `forgive()` on that loan

threats.title: AuctionHouse.forgive Forces Total Loss on Valuable Collateral When Auction Expires Without Bids

threats.hypothesis_description: LendingTerm.callMany(bytes32[]) loops over user-supplied loanIds and calls _call for each. threats.title: Debt ceiling calculation can be forced to revert via malicious gaugeWeightDelta input causing uint256(int256) conversion failure
threats.hypothesis_description: LendingTerm.debtCeiling(int256 gaugeWeightDelta) casts the adjusted gaugeWeight back to uint256: `gaugeWeight = uint256(int256(gaugeWeight) + gaugeWeightDelta)`. If gaugeWeightDelta is negative enough to make the int256 sum < 0, the uint256 cast reverts. Any caller can trigger this revert. While debtCeiling(int256) is a view function, it is directly used in critical state-changing paths (e.g., GuildToken._decrementGaugeWeight uses LendingTerm(gauge).debtCeiling(-int256(weight))). If weight is larger than current gaugeWeight, the call path can revert and block token transfers or slashing workflows that depend on decrementing weight.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical on-chain DoS primitive because the reverting cast is deterministic and easy to hit if an internal caller passes a delta that exceeds current gaugeWeight. The key operational question is whether any user-controlled paths can reach a decrement where weight > current gaugeWeight (including race conditions around gauge deprecation/re-add or preserved weights), which would then brick transfers for affected users.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gaugeWeight = uint256(int256(gaugeWeight) + gaugeWeightDelta); // revert if negative

preconditions: A code path calls debtCeiling() with gaugeWeightDelta < -currentGaugeWeight
This call is part of a required state transition (e.g., transfer freeing gauge weight, slashing, or other logic)
No prior bounds-check on gaugeWeightDelta exists in caller

initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a decrement tries to remove more weight than currently counted as gaugeWeight (state inconsistency).

threats.title: SimplePSM can burn user CREDIT for zero pegToken output due to rounding, enabling griefing via tiny redemptions and confusing accounting during stress
threats.hypothesis_description: SimplePSM.getRedeemAmountOut computes `amountOut = (amountIn * creditMultiplier) / 1e18 / decimalCorrection`. For small amountIn or low creditMultiplier, amountOut can round down to 0, yet redeem() still burns `amountIn` CREDIT and decrements pegTokenBalance by 0, effectively allowing users (or bots) to create on-chain noise and reduce their own CREDIT to 0 output. While self-harm, this can be used to spam 'redeem' events and complicate operational monitoring during incidents, and may be leveraged by integrations that assume non-zero output for non-zero input.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical correctness issue that can propagate into ops: monitoring, accounting, and integrator assumptions. During a depeg (creditMultiplier low), many small redemptions could burn credit and generate misleading signals about PSM health and user outcomes.

preconditions: amountIn is small enough (or creditMultiplier low enough) that integer division yields amountOut == 0

initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When creditMultiplier is low or pegToken has low decimals (large decimalCorrection).

threats.title: GuildVetoGovernor updateTimelock can desynchronize veto protections, allowing queued actions to become effectively unvetoable
threats.hypothesis_description: GuildVetoGovernor has a governor-controlled updateTimelock() that changes which TimelockController it can cancel from. If timelock is updated while actions are pending in the previous timelock, veto proposals for those operations may become unexecutable (because executeVeto and state() consult the new timelock address). This creates an operational window where actions in the old timelock can execute without the intended CREDIT-holder veto path, depending on how governance sequences updates.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic governance workflow hazard: timelock migrations are common, and mis-sequencing can unintentionally remove veto coverage for already-queued actions.
deployment_assumptions: Governance updates GuildVetoGovernor.timelock while there are pending operations in the previous timelock
The system relies on veto coverage for those pending operations
threats.config_evidence: No deployment/runbook evidence provided about timelock migration procedures or guards preventing mid-flight timelock changes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function updateTimelock(address newTimelock) external onlyCoreRole(GOVERNOR) { timelock = newTimelock; } // no check for pending operations in old timelock

preconditions: There exist pending operations in old timelock (getTimestamp(opId) > 1)
GuildVetoGovernor.timelock is updated to a new timelock before veto window ends
CREDIT holders attempt veto using opId from old timelock

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Veto governor timelock pointer is mutable without continuity checks
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During timelock migration or incident response

operational_questions: Is timelock migration expected, and is there a formal procedure to drain old timelock queue first?
Are there monitoring controls preventing updateTimelock while old timelock has pending ops?
Can veto governor be configured to cover multiple timelocks concurrently?
validation_requirements: Simulate: queue op in timelock A, update timelock to B, attempt create/execute veto for op in A
Review governance policies for timelock changes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Queue sensitive operation in current timelock
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance updates veto governor to new timelock before veto vote executes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Operation executes in old timelock without effective veto path

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Indirect: governance action bypasses intended veto constraint
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High-risk changes execute without veto backstop
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High; requires governance rollback if possible

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed veto executions due to wrong timelock address

threats.title: CreditToken delegateBySig uses raw ecrecover without ECDSA malleability checks, potentially enabling signature replay variants in downstream tooling
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20MultiVotes.delegateBySig uses raw ecrecover and only checks `signer != address(0)` and nonce match. It does not enforce EIP-2 low-s malleability constraints or v normalization beyond whatever ecrecover accepts. While nonce usage prevents direct replay, malleable signatures can create operational issues in off-chain tooling, signature validation libraries, and monitoring (multiple valid signatures for same message), and may open edge-case bypasses depending on wallet/provider behavior.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is less likely to be exploited directly on-chain for theft due to nonce, but can cause operational governance/voting inconsistencies and signature handling bugs in production pipelines.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address signer = ecrecover(digest, v, r, s); // no ECDSA.recover / no low-s check

preconditions: User signs delegation off-chain and signature is used on-chain
Attacker/tooling can craft alternate malleable form (high-s) that still recovers same signer under ecrecover rules (chain-dependent)
Downstream systems treat distinct signature bytes as distinct authorizations

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Raw ecrecover rather than OpenZeppelin ECDSA library

operational_questions: Do target chains enforce EIP-2 low-s at the protocol level for ecrecover precompile inputs?
Do relayers/indexers treat different signature encodings as separate intents?
Is delegateBySig used in governance automation where signature uniqueness matters?
validation_requirements: Test malleable signature variants against delegateBySig on target chain
Confirm whether ecrecover accepts high-s and how clients/wallets behave

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain a valid delegation signature from a user (legitimate workflow)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Generate alternate malleable signature encoding (if accepted)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use in off-chain systems to cause duplicate processing / monitoring bypass; on-chain nonce prevents double execution

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Off-chain vote/delegation tracking inconsistencies; possible relayer confusion
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to Medium depending on tooling

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple delegateBySig txs failing nonce checks from same signer
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Non-standard v/s values in submitted signatures

threats.title: GuildVetoGovernor description construction uses raw bytes->string conversion of timelockId, potentially generating non-UTF8 strings that can break proposal tooling or create ambiguous UI display
threats.hypothesis_description: GuildVetoGovernor._getVetoCalls builds a description string via `string(abi.encodePacked(timelockId))`. Since timelockId is arbitrary bytes32, this can produce non-UTF8 strings, truncated display, or confusing UI representations. While proposalId in OZ is computed from description hash, inconsistent UI display could cause governance participants to misinterpret which operation is being vetoed or fail to parse proposal metadata.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical governance safety concern: even if not a direct on-chain exploit, it can harm operational security by obscuring what is being vetoed. Because veto proposals are intended to be created permissionlessly, an attacker could spam confusing proposals targeting different timelockIds with non-printable bytes that appear identical in some UIs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: description = string.concat("Veto proposal for ", string(abi.encodePacked(timelockId)))

preconditions: Governance tooling relies on description string for human verification
Attacker can call createVeto/executeVeto with arbitrary timelockId values (permissionless for createVeto; execute requires proposal state constraints but callable)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bytes32 rendered as raw string, not hex

analysis_questions: Do off-chain governance tools used by the protocol properly display non-UTF8 strings?
Does proposalId depend on description text or descriptionHash (it does), and could this affect replay/uniqueness for the same timelockId?
Would changing to hex encoding be backward compatible for veto proposal creation?
validation_requirements: Test creation of veto proposals with timelockIds containing non-printable bytes and observe UI behavior
Confirm whether any monitoring/alerting parses description strings and could be bypassed

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker creates veto proposals with timelockIds that render as confusing strings, potentially causing voters to ignore legitimate veto attempts or misidentify the target operation in off-chain tooling.
prerequisites: Users rely on UI text rather than decoding calldata

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect governance risk (missed veto leading to execution of harmful timelock action)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational mitigation via better tooling; contract change would require governance

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for description formatting / UI display safety

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor veto proposal descriptions for non-hex / non-printable characters

threats.title: forgive() on Active Auction Permanently Bricks AuctionHouse
threats.hypothesis_description: If governance calls `forgive()` on a loan that is currently in an active auction, the loan is closed, but the auction remains active. The auction can never be settled, permanently inflating `nAuctionsInProgress` and blocking `setAuctionHouse`.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `forgive()` function sets `loans[loanId].closeTime = block.timestamp` but does not check if `callTime != 0` (i.e., the loan is in auction). If an auction is active, `AuctionHouse.bid()` or `AuctionHouse.forgive()` will eventually call `LendingTerm.onBid()`. `onBid` requires `closeTime == 0`, so it reverts. The auction state is permanently stuck, and `nAuctionsInProgress` never decrements. `setAuctionHouse` requires `nAuctionsInProgress == 0`, so the auction house can never be upgraded.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `forgive()` sets `closeTime` without checking or clearing auction state

preconditions: A loan is called and an auction is started
Governance calls `forgive()` on the loan before the auction concludes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State transition bypasses expected lifecycle checks

operational_questions: Is governance expected to use `forgive` on active auctions?
Can `nAuctionsInProgress` be manually corrected?
validation_requirements: Start an auction
Call `forgive` as governor
Attempt to call `bid` and observe the revert
Verify `setAuctionHouse` reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: An operational emergency occurs, and governance decides to forgive a loan that is currently in auction.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The loan closes, but the auction is orphaned.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The protocol is permanently unable to upgrade the `AuctionHouse`.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to upgrade core protocol components (`AuctionHouse`)
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires `emergencyAction` to manually edit storage slots
